summary: I was born in Sherman, Texas, in 1909. My father worked as a city clerk there during the first city manager government in Texas, which Sherman claimed alongside Galveston to have originated. My great-aunt and great-uncle, who had a business in Sherman, raised me and my sister after my mother died.
cues: first city manager government in Texas

summary: My mother was a second-generation immigrant from England, and her family had all been miners. This English mining heritage contrasted with my husband's family, who were miners in the United States. Her background influenced some of my views growing up.

My mother and my husband's family met and married in Chicago, where they lived for eleven years before she died. This connection linked my English immigrant mother and my husband's American mining family.

I spent much of my life in Fort Worth, Texas, where my family returned when I was eleven. My great-aunt had been a teacher in the first public schools there after Reconstruction. Later, my husband moved to Fort Worth before I did, and we were active in the Baptist Church in that city.

My great-aunt taught in the first public schools in Fort Worth after the Reconstruction Era. This period shaped the educational opportunities available to her and influenced my upbringing through her example. The legacy of Reconstruction was part of the historical context of my family.

Lua Dial was my great-aunt who played a principal role in my youth. She was one of the first graduates of the first normal school at Huntsville and taught in Fort Worth's early public schools. She helped raise me and my sister after our mother died.

Preston Drury Hollingsworth was my great-uncle who, along with Lua Dial, raised me and my sister. He had a business in Sherman, Texas, and was a significant influence on my youth. Their household was central to my upbringing after my mother's death.

My great-aunt was born the year the American Civil War began, and my great-grandfather fought as a general in the Army of the Confederate States of America. Benjamin Franklin Dial was my great-grandfather and a general in the Army of the Confederate States of America. entity: Army of the Confederate States of America
summary: My great-grandfather served as a general in the Army of the Confederate States of America during the Civil War. Marshall, Texas, was where my great-grandparents lived and was considered an outpost of Anglo-American civilization at the time. Many of their old friends lived there, and it was a significant place in my family's history. The town's culture influenced the perspectives I inherited.

There was some Quaker influence in my family through my mother's older brother, who became a Friends minister. This influence shaped some of my mother's views, which I inherited, especially regarding race and social justice. The Quaker connection contrasted with other Southern attitudes I encountered.

Thurber, Texas, was a company town near Stephenville where my husband's family lived. It was centered around the Thurber Coal Mine and the Thurber Brick Company. My husband was born in Stephenville because that was where they had to go to get a doctor, but he spent his early years in Thurber.

The Thurber Coal Mine was one of the main employers in the company town of Thurber, Texas, where my husband's family worked. My husband and I were quite active in the Baptist Church, which is where we met. He originally expected to become a Baptist minister until his experiences at Baylor University changed his perspective. The church was central to our early relationship and community life.

Texas is where I was born, raised, and spent most of my life, including Sherman, Fort Worth, and other towns. My family's history, including my great-grandparents and great-aunt, is deeply rooted in Texas. The political and social landscape of Texas shaped much of my personal and political development.

By 1940, my husband and I were appalled at how the local establishment abused the New Deal program. We helped organize a county convention supporting Roosevelt and the New Deal, opposing Garner's faction. We believed the New Deal was dedicated to improving the lot of the poorest people, despite local resistance.

In 1940, we cooperated with Maury Maverick Sr. to organize our county convention in support of Roosevelt and the New Deal. This collaboration was part of our effort to counter the local political machine's opposition. Maverick was an important ally in our political activism.

I became an enthusiastic supporter of Roosevelt after his election and the first hundred days of his presidency. Despite initial skepticism, I believed in his leadership and the New Deal's potential to help the poor. My husband and I worked politically to sustain support for Roosevelt in our county.

During the 1940 county convention, we worked to support Roosevelt without continuing support for Vice President John Nance Garner. Garner was part of the local political establishment we opposed. Our efforts aimed to shift delegate support away from Garner's faction.

The 1932 presidential election was my first to vote in, and I did not vote for Roosevelt but for Norman Thomas, the socialist candidate. At the time, Roosevelt's campaign did not seem very different from Hoover's to me. This election marked the beginning of my political awareness.

I voted for Norman Thomas, the socialist candidate, in the 1932 election, which was unusual in Fort Worth. I have kept that vote somewhat private because of the stigma attached. It was a personal choice reflecting my early political views before becoming more involved in Democratic politics.

I voted for John Tower in the 1961 Texas United States Senate special election, seeing him as the lesser of two evils compared to Blakley. Tower was a Republican, and my vote was unusual for a Texas Democrat, but I felt he was easier to remove later. Unfortunately, I was mistaken in that judgment.

entity: 1961 Texas United States Senate special election

I once voted for a relatively impecunious teacher from Wichita Falls in a political context, seeing him as a preferable candidate to a wealthy opponent.
